text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through the palmar point and places lateral ports, revealing extensive omental and bowel adhesions from the xiphoid process to the pelvis. Adhesiolysis begins with sharp scissor dissection, staying close to the abdominal wall while pneumoperitoneum aids in creating space through pneumodissection.
The surgeon uses harmonic shears to perform adhesiolysis, staying close to the anterior abdominal wall. Small herniated defects and previous sutures become visible during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully applies gentle traction and counter-traction with rotatory movements to reduce the bowel contents through a large hernia defect. The underlying bowel, previously sutured, is visible, and care is taken to avoid sudden movements to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon prepares to control any bleeding using bipolar or harmonic shears, while adjusting trochars and ports to maintain optimal access and visibility within the surgical field.

The surgeon observes the bowel densely adhering to the anterior abdominal wall, using sharp cutting scissors to carefully dissect without applying any energy source near the bowel. The previous sutures are visible, showing dense adherence of the bowel to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs slow and meticulous dissection during the adhesiolysis, carefully separating dense adhesions between the bowel and abdominal wall, ensuring precision to avoid damage to underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the herniated defect during adhesiolysis. Several entrotomies are visible, which are addressed laparoscopically with immediate suturing and repair.

I would like to emphasize that while if there is some endotomy that is encountered, so just stop with the IDC lysis and then do the repair immediately. The reason being is we do not lose track of the enterotomy and then a proper traction which is there by the previous additions can be obtained. Then after closing the enterotomy, I continue the edysulisis.

Another hernia defect is visualized, and the surgeon reduces the herniated contents completely. The adhesions are visible, extending from the upper to lower regions, with the defects clearly observable.

The surgeon observes the dense adhesions of the bowel to the abdominal wall caused by previous sutures. A large enterotomy is visible, and the surgeon uses 3-0 Vicryl to close the enterotomy defect with intermittent sutures, ensuring shallow bites to prevent future bowel strictures.
The surgeon continues sharp adhesiolysis, carefully dissecting adhesions as the procedure progresses towards the pelvis. Two enterotomies have been encountered and addressed during the process.

The panoramic view shows the completed adhesiolysis with visible Swiss cheese defects and previous suture bites on the underlying bowel. The surgeon uses non-absorbable barbed sutures to close the defect, avoiding mesh placement due to the presence of enterotomies.

in these cases a laparoscopic anatomical repair of the hernia would suffice and we have to explain to the patients that in the case of recurrence we might put a mesh and that is the very prudent and a very important decision that has to be explained to the patient and to the patient's attenders.
The surgeon completes the closure of the hernia defects and inspects the surgical area for hemostasis, marking the end of the procedure.
